author: taulepton_
content: Thanks for the feedback/comments <@427491045308235776> <@845691100072509460>  🙏 I have provided response. Based on <@845691100072509460> comments, I recognise the proof re-signing is slightly unclear / under-defined so I will put together a short note / writeup to describe the problem we are trying to solve and how proof re-signing attempts to address the issue. The intention is that we can use the note to align on the problem and the proposed solution and then proceed with discussion from a basis of mutual understanding.

author: taulepton_
content:
Replied in gh but copying my response here:
```
The NewPayloadRequestRoot can be derived from NewPayloadRequestHeader.hash_tree_root() or NewPayloadRequest.hash_tree_root() (they are identical due to ssz). In future iterations, when we no longer gossip the full beacon block, we will migrate to NewPayloadRequestHeader.hash_tree_root(), but for now, it's unnecessary as nodes receive the full beacon block.
```

author: taulepton_
content:
<@427491045308235776> do you have any thoughts about this?
```
Add proof_types to ExecutionProofStatus as a dynamic capability advertisement, so peers can communicate which proof types they support without requiring a hard fork or new client release. This enables new proof types to be rolled out and adopted incrementally. (I'm most uncertain about this as it may have implications for consensus)
```

author: kevaundray
content: More writeups == good 😄

author: kevaundray
content: Trying to think of a usecase for this 🤔

author: taulepton_
content: The use case would be shipping new guests / verifier keys without requiring new client releases / social orchestration

author: kevaundray
content: How would we coordinate with other nodes in order to ship the new guest / verifier key?

author: kevaundray
content: Thinking about the case where I add a new guest, but noone else does for example

author: taulepton_
content: The idea is that a node would update its config to introduce the new guest. When two nodes exchange the ExecutionProofStatus message, they exchange information about which guests they support. Using this information, they can decide which proofs to communicate. Additionally, it could be used for peer selection, i.e. only maintain connections with peers that support the same guests as you.

author: taulepton_
content: A node could, in theory, support both old and new guests. For peers who only support old guests, they should communicate the proofs for those guests. With peers who support the new guests, they can gossip the new.

author: taulepton_
content: I would consider it a means of dynamically communicating guest support amongst peers.

author: kevaundray
content:
This makes sense, just trying to think through the usecases for when this would be used because a new guest would need to be added in both the CL (attester) and the block builder/prover. 

If I'm the only one who supports `reth-v0.1` for example and everyone/the majority is on `reth-v0.2` then as a prover, I think I would be incentivised to only create proofs for `reth-v0.2`

author: kevaundray
content: Maybe orthogonal, but I'm wondering how we would make this work for historical proofs - where the version has changed

author: kevaundray
content: Theres also this small note on updating between hardforks (due to bugs) which may be a reason we should have this: https://hackmd.io/@kevaundray/B1dE392-Ze

author: taulepton_
content: Yes, that's correct. I guess the use case could be that there are multiple provers on the network. One of the provers has just produced a more efficient guest, and they want to test it out on a live network with a subset of peers. They collaborate with that subset and distribute the verifier keys, the subset updates their CL configs, and they proceed with the live network tests. The other remaining provers continue generating proofs with the old guests.

author: taulepton_
content: Yes this may also be a potential use case

author: taulepton_
content: Tbh, I'm not 100% sure on the exact use case, but I get the impression that guest support may be dynamic over time, and we should have a way to communicate proof type/guest support. I think the bug fix is probably the most compelling use case.

author: taulepton_
content: I think the key question we need to answer here is, do we want guest support to be tightly coupled to hard forks?

author: kevaundray
content: I'm thinking that in production, we probably do because we are essentially changing the stf of the node, which ideally is done with heavy scrutiny -- perhaps we can see how things pan out before adding it?

author: kevaundray
content: Though also thinking about the bug fix problem and how to solve it

author: taulepton_
content: Yes, that is true. I tend to think that dynamic guests are more useful during the optional proofs phase, when we are likely to iterate on guests more frequently (and proofs are not consensus-impacting). During mandatory proofs, I'm less certain, but the bug fix use case may be a good fit.

author: taulepton_
content: I propose we ponder this over the next day or so. We don't need to reach a conclusion on this immediately.

author: kevaundray
content:
yep agreed -- in the hackmd, I also noted that we could have a indicator to say "I will switch to `reth-v0.9`  in N blocks" -- this is so that provers can have enough time to add the new guest (theres a bug in the previous one) allowing for a smooth transition. 

Do you mind sparing a thought for this too and just the mechanism in general outlined?

author: taulepton_
content: Certainly

author: kevaundray
content: On this, I was hoping we could frontload 8025 with most of the changes needed but yep this is correct -- can we add an issue to update BiB to make this change? <@845691100072509460> was also planning to make some changes to BiB so we could possibly do it in one go

author: taulepton_
content: sure thing, should I add that in consensus specs repo or the eip repo?

author: kevaundray
content: I think consensus-specs since we removed NewPayloadRequestHeader from there

author: taulepton_
content: on it

author: kevaundray
content: thank you ser 🙏
